chapters.title: Imperialism runs deep inside British identity
chapters.text:
Britain's colonial record is woven into almost every part of modern life. Beyond the statues of empire-builders, the schools named after them, and the apologies that never quite land, the reach of empire is far more ordinary. Take the Earl of Meath, who pushed hard for a holiday that would honor British conquests. He won in 1916, when the government agreed to mark Empire Day. The name changed to Commonwealth Day in 1958, but the original mission of celebrating colonialism still echoes. A new version of Empire Day could redirect attention toward how empire shaped Britain itself.

Little traces surface all the time. The Hobson-Jobson Dictionary records that a dam was once a tiny fraction of a rupee, considered worthless. British officials in India used the phrase "don't give a dumri," which eventually became "I don't give a damn." In business, Marcus Samuel built his fortune importing shells from Asia and turned it into the oil company now known as Shell. Wembley Stadium started life as the Empire Stadium, raised for the 1924 Empire Exhibition.

John Cabot set sail from Bristol in 1497 and laid the early groundwork for what would grow into the empire.

London, Glasgow, and nearly every big city in Britain carry imperial history in their streets and buildings.

Free school meals trace back to the empire. Some historians say the welfare state grew mostly from a political need to protect imperial interests and keep the public calm. The food on British tables is part of the story too, since many common dishes began life in the colonies. Even the Royal Family is less British than people assume. The family has foreign roots, and Queen Victoria spoke German. She held the title Empress of India.

chapters.title: The empire reshaped nearly the whole world
chapters.text:
British diversity comes from centuries of ruling almost a quarter of the planet. Honors tied to the British Empire are still handed out to deserving people, and imperial habits shape nearly every area of daily life. Almost no corner of the globe is untouched by British influence. For many people, the idea of Brexit is absurd, because Britain cannot stand apart from the rest of the world.

The empire made London the center of global finance and built wealth for cities, institutions, and families across the country. The imprint of imperialism is so deep that pulling down a few statues or changing a few names will not erase it.

The Sikh community has a striking history with the empire. A memorial in Amritsar honors 21 Sikh soldiers who died at the Battle of Saragarhi on September 12, 1897. They gave their lives, and the House of Parliament praised their courage. Sikhs are often held up as a model of successful cultural blending, though they remain one of the few examples.

The stories of non-white soldiers who fought for Britain tend to disappear from view. School history skips the famines that struck India.

On April 13, 1919, the Jallianwala Bagh massacre shook Amritsar. General Reginald Dyer ordered his men to fire on a crowd of nearly 20,000 people gathered in a park. Official reports counted 379 dead, but historian Kim Wagner believes the real number is close to a thousand. Other acts of racism against Indians, including assaults, job discrimination, and housing bias, fill the record. Studying imperial history shows why so many Indians and Sikhs ended up in Britain and how those old wounds shape life today.

Sports like cricket, polo, football, racquet games, and snooker spread worldwide through the empire.

Britain claims credit for the English language, fashion, literature, judges' wigs, parliamentary politics, and the Anglican Church. The harm done in the process must be faced too. In Iraq, Nigeria, Sudan, Hong Kong, Kashmir, and Burma, imperial damage still fuels the conflicts that tear at those places.

Research has shown that at its largest, the British Empire covered 13.71 million square miles, which was about 24 percent of the world's land.

chapters.title: Some chapters of British colonial history are hard to pin down
chapters.text:
Historians put the empire's lifespan somewhere between 400 and 500 years. At its peak it included 10,000 islands, 2,000 rivers, 1,000 lakes, 500 capes, 100 peninsulas, and an entire continent. Many books have told the violent side of that record. Adolf Hitler pointed to Britain's control of nearly half a billion Indians when he imagined what Germany could become.

The East India Company played a major role in building the empire.

The East India Company offered a cover for British exploration of foreign lands, especially when that work was awkward or broke British law. After gaining trading rights and control of sea routes, the company received a new charter that let it claim, fortify, and settle overseas territories. The story starts in 1667, when the Dutch and the British settled a dispute over islands in modern-day Indonesia, and Britain walked away with Manhattan. One small island declared loyalty to the English while its neighbors accepted Dutch rule. The island was called Pulo Run, or Run for short. It was tiny, but its nutmeg groves could turn a profit of roughly 32,000 percent when shipped to Europe. Since the company could not officially hold foreign land, it accepted Run's pledge in the name of King James I in 1616. When the Dutch caught on, they blockaded the island for years. In the end, the company traded its rights to Run for New Amsterdam on the North American coast. That city grew into New York.

The deal over Run opened the door to British control of territory across the world. There is no firm agreement on when the empire began, though Run is a reasonable starting point. There is also no agreement on when it ended. Suggested dates range from the Punjab massacre in 1919 to the Suez crisis in 1956. Some argue that the empire lives on in the British Overseas Territories.

chapters.title: How Britain ruled varied widely across its territories
chapters.text:
British India was governed by a viceroy who answered to London. Other colonies were handled in different ways, depending on which phase of empire they fell into.

The first phase ran from the 1600s to the 1780s and had no single authority. Companies and private individuals ran affairs, covering the American colonies and the Caribbean. The second phase focused on India and Africa, and the East India Company was the main power. The company printed its own money, fielded an army twice the size of the regular British army, set taxes, and operated out of unmarked buildings in London.

In 1858, the East India Company lost its grip, and the British state took over. Even so, control was uneven. Some regions were held tight, others loosely. In places like Egypt and Latin America, Britain leaned on financial muscle rather than soldiers.

While the empire grew into Africa in the 1870s, it collapsed in America and weakened in the Caribbean.

Britain's identity shifted with global trends. It moved from profiting off the slave trade to seeing itself as a civilizing force.

Between 1660 and 1807, Britain earned vast sums from the Atlantic slave trade, shipping around 3 million Africans to the Americas. After Parliament banned slavery, Britain took a leading role in ending it elsewhere.

The cultural stance of the empire was uneven too. Some Britons adopted the religion, food, and habits of the people they ruled. A few East India Company employees embraced Hindu customs and honored Indian gods. That openness faded in the early 20th century, and social circles separated.

Imperialism had its fans and its critics in Britain. Supporters included Rudyard Kipling, Joseph Chamberlain, Lord Rosebery, Cecil Rhodes, Lord Meath, and Benjamin Disraeli. The loudest critic was four-time Prime Minister William Gladstone, who condemned the killing of the Zulus and the violence in Afghanistan in 1879.

In the mid-1800s, Britons often used a vile racial slur to refer to Indians.

chapters.title: Many colonized lands had their treasures taken
chapters.text:
The English word "loot" comes from the Hindi word "lut," meaning spoils of war, and entered common use in the 1850s. Goods seized by imperial forces sit in British museums to this day.

Colonel Sir Francis Younghusband led the British expedition that plundered Tibet in 1903. Traveling with him was archaeologist Dr. Laurence Waddell, who was fascinated by Tibetan culture but had little respect for it.

Lord Curzon defended Britain's seizure of Tibet by claiming it opened a military path to China and blocked Russian access. The mission failed politically, but it fed Waddell's scholarly curiosity and filled imperial collections. Painted scrolls, amber, diamonds, golden crowns, and tiger skins made their way back to Britain. Official policy banned the taking of war spoils, with a loophole that allowed seizure and public auction of goods taken from resisting locals.

Scientists, including cartographers, archaeologists, biologists, and linguists, traveled into conquered territories to gather objects for research or museum display. Soldiers also picked up curiosities as souvenirs, often taking as much as they liked.

Waddell used a government budget of 10,000 rupees to haul away 400 mule loads of artifacts, texts, weapons, porcelain, armor, and paintings. He struck unfair bargains and sometimes did not pay at all.

Most of the items in the British Museum were taken, stolen, or bought at unfair prices from colonized peoples.

Three Indian soldiers died while trying to break open a locked box they thought held treasure. The box held gunpowder, and the spark from their attempt set it off.

Pillaging was a normal part of warfare and counted as part of a soldier's pay, with how much someone took depending on regimental culture and personal morals.

After Western troops stormed the Chinese Imperial Summer Palace in 1860, they found a Pekingese dog and named it Looty. They gave Looty to Queen Victoria, who auctioned the dog for the modern equivalent of around 18.9 million pounds.

chapters.title: Britain credits the empire for its modern diversity
chapters.text:
Sake Dean Mahomed, born in 1759 and died in 1851, was the first writer from India to publish in English. He came of age in Patna, a city split between English, French, and Dutch influence. Through the workings of the empire, he landed work as a camp follower with the English Company's Bengal Army, serving under Godfrey Baker, who brought him to Ireland. Mahomed penned a series of letters under the title Travels, ran off with teenager Jane Daly after his Anglo-Irish patron died in 1786, brought curry houses to Britain, helped make massage popular, and paved the way for later non-white writers to find a readership. What stands out about Mahomed is that his whole life shows how the empire made modern multicultural Britain.

Britain's cultural and racial mix comes straight from the mix in its old colonies.

Native Americans ended up in England because of empire ties. Indians who served the East India Company trained at Haileybury College and Addiscombe Military Seminary. Some enslaved people, called Black Loyalists, won their freedom by fighting for the British in the American War of Independence. Even after laws banned it, some landowners still kept Black people in bondage. Imperialism explains why non-white doctors worked in Britain centuries ago, and why Hindus from Gujarat, Muslims from Pakistan and Bangladesh, and Sikhs from Punjab settled in Britain after the Second World War. Forecasts suggest that ethnic minorities could make up a third of the British population by 2050.

Britain tends to skip the empire's role in shaping modern diversity. School programs leave out British colonial history, and a kind of imperial amnesia has settled over the country. That gap in education has pushed some people to harass immigrants and tell them to go back home. Yet Britons originally invited those immigrants in to fill post-war labor gaps. Facing this chapter honestly is critical today.

Debates about multiculturalism will lose their sting once Britain admits the role empire played in creating it.

Research from the University of Manchester shows that white Britons form a minority of the population in Luton, Slough, and Leicester.

chapters.title: Wrapping up
chapters.text:
Britain's imperial past shapes the United Kingdom today. Recently, much debate has focused on the economic legacies of empire. Some workers have resigned from museums that refuse to return objects taken from other countries by force. Statues of imperialists, racists, and segregationists are coming down.

Pulling down these statues does not erase history, despite what some claim. Nazi history did not vanish when Berlin renamed its streets. The same approach can apply here. We can study the past and still recognize the empire's wrongs.

Imperial legacies run far deeper than most people realize. Museums need to show integrity, and society needs to accept multiculturalism as an inheritance from the empire.

Even if we cannot divide imperial history into simple good and evil, we must hold it accountable for what it left behind. Remembering the genocides and the systems of white supremacy, and making amends where possible, is essential. Facing this history is the only way to move forward.

The empire lives on in language, art, food, financial institutions, and the tradition of anti-racism and internationalism that laid the foundations of the welfare state. Just as Black History Month sets aside time for reflection, Britain needs to set aside time to reconsider its imperial record. Schools need to tell the truth about imperialism. That education will raise awareness of the many failures of the British Empire.

Take some time to test your own knowledge of imperial history. How has colonization touched your life in ways you may not have noticed? Pick one practical step you can take to push the British government to acknowledge its imperial past. Ask minorities and immigrants to share their stories, and use what you learn to rethink your own assumptions.
